pageName: TiDB vs CockroachDB - 2026 Comparison Guide for Platform Teams

meta.title: TiDB vs CockroachDB - 2026: Comparison Guide
meta.description: Compare TiDB vs CockroachDB on compatibility, ACID transactions, scaling, HTAP analytics, multi-region tradeoffs, ops, and pricing—then pick the best fit.

props.content:
**TL;DR:** TiDB and CockroachDB are both distributed SQL databases built for horizontal scale, but they differ fundamentally in compatibility, analytics capability, and operational model. TiDB offers MySQL compatibility, built-in HTAP analytics via TiFlash, and elastic scaling on TiKV without manual sharding—ideal for teams migrating from MySQL and needing real-time analytics. CockroachDB targets Postgres ecosystems with strong multi-region write resilience but requires external tools for analytics. Choose TiDB if you value MySQL compatibility and want HTAP out of the box; choose CockroachDB if your team is Postgres-native and prioritizes cross-region write availability.

**Updated:** February 2026 | **Author:** PingCAP Content Team | **Reviewed by:** Database Architecture Team

# TiDB vs CockroachDB: Side-by-Side Comparison

| **Comparison Criteria** | **TiDB** | **CockroachDB** |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Use** | OLTP + HTAP (real-time analytics on transactional data via TiFlash columnar replica); multi-region read-heavy and mixed workloads; MySQL-compatible applications at scale | OLTP with geographic distribution; read-heavy multi-region deployments; Postgres-compatible applications requiring resilience and survivability |
| **SQL / Protocol Compatibility** | MySQL compatible—wire protocol, SQL dialect, and most MySQL drivers work without modification; not 100% feature-parity (some functions, spatial types require testing) | Postgres wire-compatible; psql, pgAdmin, and Postgres drivers work; SQL subset compatible, but full Postgres feature parity not guaranteed |
| **Consistency & Distributed Transactions** | ACID transactions with serializable isolation via Percolator-inspired two-phase commit; distributed transaction coordination ensures consistency across TiKV nodes; lock-free reads on TiFlash replicas | ACID transactions with serializable isolation via intent-based locking; distributed transactions with automatic retry and deadlock resolution; strong consistency guarantees across all replicas |
| **Scaling Model** | Horizontal scaling without manual sharding—TiDB compute nodes are stateless and can be added freely; TiKV (distributed storage layer) and TiFlash (columnar store) scale independently via range-based partitioning managed transparently | Horizontal scaling with automatic range-based sharding and rebalancing; all nodes participate in consensus; scale-out adds both compute and storage |
| **HTAP Database Capability** | Native HTAP via TiFlash—columnar replica updated via TiKV Raft log; OLTP queries on row store, OLAP queries route to TiFlash with sub-second consistency; no separate ETL pipeline required | Requires external OLAP systems (Kafka + data warehouse, materialized views, or third-party connectors); no built-in columnar store; analytics queries run on main OLTP cluster at risk of interference |
| **Multi-Region Database Patterns** | Single-region HA (3+ replicas in one DC); cross-region read replicas (async follower reads, low-latency for reads); multi-region writes require cross-region consensus and incur latency penalties; leader-follower topology common | Multi-region active-active with automatic failover; all regions participate in Raft consensus; write latency increases with geographic distance; enterprise plans support survival zones and staggered regions |
| **Operations & Reliability** | Self-managed: operator-friendly (Kubernetes-native via TiDB Operator); TiDB Cloud (managed) handles provisioning, patches, backups; online DDL (schema changes with zero downtime); no manual downtime for upgrades or node replacements | Self-managed: CockroachDB Operator available; CockroachDB Serverless (managed) available; online schema changes supported; rolling upgrades without downtime |
| **Implementation / Migration Surface Area** | MySQL schema + application compatibility testing required; data migration tools (TiDB Lightning, DM) available; application connection strings usually work as-is; testing needed for: complex transactions, triggers, stored procedures, time-zone handling | Postgres schema translation needed; application changes likely for Postgres-specific features; data migration tools available; connection strings may require updates; Postgres feature gaps require refactoring |
| **Pricing Model & Cost Drivers** | **TiDB Cloud**: pay-per-node compute, per-GB storage, per-GB backup, egress; **Self-managed**: infrastructure + operational overhead. Cost drivers: cluster size, multi-region replicas, TiFlash (columnar) overhead, backup retention, support tier | **CockroachDB Cloud**: per-vCPU-hour, per-GB storage, per-GB egress, backup; **Self-managed**: infrastructure + licensing. Cost drivers: geographic redundancy, cluster size, request volume, multi-region consensus latency (affects throughput), support tier |

---

## Key Takeaways

- **MySQL compatibility vs Postgres**: TiDB works with existing MySQL apps and drivers; CockroachDB requires Postgres ecosystem tooling and often needs application changes.
- **HTAP & analytics**: TiDB includes TiFlash (columnar replica) for real-time analytics; CockroachDB requires external analytics stack (Kafka, data warehouse, or third-party tools).
- **Operational model**: TiDB Cloud and CockroachDB Serverless both offer managed options; self-managed TiDB uses Kubernetes operators; both support online schema change (zero-downtime DDL).
- **Multi-region**: TiDB excels at multi-region **reads** (async replicas, low latency); multi-region **writes** incur cross-region consensus overhead; CockroachDB supports active-active multi-region but all writes cross geographic boundaries.
- **Scaling**: TiDB separates compute (stateless) from storage (TiKV) and analytics (TiFlash), allowing independent scale; CockroachDB couples compute and storage, requiring both to grow together.

## What This Guide Covers

Quickly navigate the key comparison areas between TiDB and CockroachDB:

- **[Overview](#overview)** — What each database is and when this comparison matters
- **[Key Differences](#key-differences)** — Critical decision drivers for buyers
- **[Architecture](#architecture)** — How TiDB's compute-storage separation and TiKV differ from CockroachDB's design
- **[MySQL Compatibility](#compatibility)** — Drop-in replacement potential and migration surface area
- **[Consistency & Transactions](#consistency)** — ACID transactions and distributed transaction handling
- **[Scaling & Performance](#scaling)** — Horizontal scaling models, database partitioning, and database sharding realities
- **[HTAP / Analytics](#htap)** — TiFlash columnar engine vs external analytics patterns
- **[Multi-Region Database](#multi-region)** — Latency, locality, and write-path tradeoffs
- **[Operations & Reliability](#operations)** — Online DDL, upgrades, backups, and day-2 complexity
- **[Ecosystem & Integrations](#ecosystem)** — Deployment, data movement, and tooling
- **[Implementation / Migration](#migration)** — De-risking your move in 5 steps
- **[Pricing](#pricing)** — Cost drivers and total cost of ownership comparison
- **[Pros & Cons](#pros-cons)** — Neutral tradeoff summary for each
- **[Decision Matrix](#decision-matrix)** — Who should choose which database
- **[FAQs](#faqs)** — Answers to the most common questions

## TiDB vs CockroachDB: 2-Minute Overview

**TiDB** is a distributed SQL database built on **TiKV**, a distributed key-value storage layer, that delivers MySQL compatibility with horizontal scaling and ACID transactions across regions. **TiFlash** adds real-time columnar analytics (HTAP database capability) without requiring separate ETL pipelines. TiDB eliminates manual database sharding through automatic data partitioning while preserving the SQL and transaction semantics teams expect from traditional databases.

**CockroachDB** is a NewSQL database with Postgres wire compatibility, designed for geo-distributed deployments and high availability. It emphasizes resilience and multi-region write capability but requires more operational familiarity with its distributed architecture and different transaction model.

### When This Comparison Matters

You're outgrowing a single-node SQL database or monolithic architecture and need:
- **Distributed SQL** that scales horizontally without manual sharding overhead
- **ACID transactions** across multiple nodes and regions
- **Multi-region deployment** with acceptable latency and consistency tradeoffs
- **HTAP analytics** (real-time OLTP + OLAP on fresh data) to simplify your data stack
- **MySQL compatibility** to minimize application migration effort

If your team is already invested in Postgres and prefers its ecosystem, or if you're deploying in a strictly single-region HA model, the decision tree shifts. This guide compares both on architecture, consistency, scaling, multi-region realities, operational overhead, and pricing—so you can choose the right fit for your workload and team.

## Key Decision Drivers for BOFU Evaluators

When comparing TiDB and CockroachDB, platform engineers and database architects should focus on five critical decision drivers that directly impact production outcomes:

- **MySQL Compatibility vs Postgres Ecosystem**: TiDB is MySQL-compatible, allowing teams to leverage existing MySQL tooling, drivers (mysql2, mysqlnd), and ORMs (Sequelize, Doctrine) with minimal refactoring. CockroachDB targets the Postgres ecosystem, requiring validation of Postgres wire-protocol compatibility and potential rewrites for Postgres-specific features. This choice cascades through your application stack and team skill sets.

- **HTAP Analytics Path: TiFlash vs External Systems**: TiDB includes TiFlash, a columnar replica engine that powers real-time OLAP workloads alongside transactional workloads on the same cluster, eliminating separate ETL pipelines and analytical databases for many use cases. CockroachDB does not include built-in columnar storage, instead requiring external analytics tools (Snowflake, Redshift, BigQuery) or materialized views, adding operational complexity and increasing time-to-insight.

- **Partitioning Model vs Operational Reality**: TiDB abstracts away manual database sharding—the cluster automatically distributes and partitions data across regions and nodes without app-level routing logic. CockroachDB's partitioning and zone configurations require explicit operational planning, adding complexity for teams unfamiliar with distributed SQL partition management.

- **Distributed Transactions and Consistency Guarantees**: Both systems provide ACID transactions across nodes, but TiDB uses a percolator-inspired consensus model optimized for high throughput, while CockroachDB uses Raft-based consensus. Teams must validate transaction latency profiles, conflict handling, and retry behavior under their specific contention patterns during POC testing.

- **Multi-Region Database Latency and Write-Path Locality**: TiDB's architecture allows reads and writes to scale to any region independently when replica groups are placed strategically, but cross-region writes incur consensus latency. CockroachDB's Raft consensus model prioritizes consistency across regions but may introduce higher latency for geographically distant write coordination. Teams must measure actual cross-region round-trip times and validate against SLOs during design review.

- **Self-Managed Infrastructure vs Managed Service Trade-offs**: Self-managed TiDB deployments (on Kubernetes or bare metal) provide cost control and full customization but require in-house expertise for upgrades, monitoring, and day-2 operations. TiDB Cloud offers a managed alternative, reducing operational overhead. CockroachDB also offers both self-managed and managed (CockroachCloud) options, each with different staffing and compliance assumptions.

Each driver influences the total cost of ownership, time-to-production, and operational risk profile. Use these as a lens when evaluating proof-of-concept results and vendor demos.

## TiDB vs CockroachDB: Architecture and Real-World Implications

The architectural choices in TiDB and CockroachDB shape how they handle latency, failure domains, and rebalancing—three critical factors for production workloads.

### TiDB: Compute-Storage Separation with TiKV and TiFlash

TiDB decouples compute from storage, a design pattern that enables independent scaling and operational flexibility:

- **TiKV as distributed KV storage**: TiKV serves as TiDB's distributed key-value storage layer, handling OLTP operations with Raft-based replication across nodes. This separation means compute nodes (TiDB servers) are stateless and can scale horizontally without reshuffling data.
- **TiFlash columnar replica for HTAP**: TiFlash provides a columnar replica of TiKV data, optimized for analytical queries. Updates flow from TiKV to TiFlash asynchronously, allowing real-time OLTP + OLAP analytics on the same cluster without external ETL.
- **Impact on latency and failure domains**: Compute-storage separation isolates database node failures from data availability. If a TiDB node fails, queries route to other compute nodes; storage remains unaffected. Cross-region replication in TiKV is managed independently of compute scaling.
- **Rebalancing behavior**: When nodes join or leave, TiKV rebalances data (Raft regions) in the background without blocking writes. TiFlash follows, ensuring analytical replicas stay current.

### CockroachDB: Distributed SQL Design

CockroachDB embeds storage and consensus into each node, making every node a full database replica:

- **Unified SQL + storage + Raft**: Every CockroachDB node runs SQL parsing, execution, and storage together. Data is replicated via Raft across all nodes in the cluster, creating a homogeneous architecture.
- **Write amplification and cross-region cost**: Because every write must pass through Raft consensus, multi-region clusters incur higher latency for write-heavy workloads. Strong consistency requires a quorum of replicas to acknowledge each write, which in geo-distributed setups means waiting for inter-region round trips.
- **Rebalancing and range splitting**: CockroachDB automatically splits data ranges as they grow and rebalances them across nodes. This works efficiently in single-region clusters but adds complexity in multi-region deployments where cross-region data movement increases network traffic.

### What Architecture Means for Real Workloads

**Latency**
- **TiDB**: Compute-storage separation can reduce tail latency on large analytical queries because they run on dedicated compute without blocking OLTP. Multi-region setups benefit from TiFlash locality—analytical queries run against local columnar replicas.
- **CockroachDB**: Every node runs the full database stack, so adding compute capacity doesn't isolate expensive queries. In multi-region deployments, write latency is often determined by the furthest replica in the quorum.

**Failure domains**
- **TiDB**: Failure of one TiDB compute node removes capacity but doesn't risk data loss (TiKV handles replication). Failure of a TiKV node triggers Raft recovery and rebalancing, independent of compute restarts.
- **CockroachDB**: Each node is a full replica, so node failures impact both availability and the rebalancing workload. Recovering a failed node means re-streaming all data from peers, which can saturate network bandwidth in large clusters.

**Rebalancing**
- **TiDB**: TiKV rebalances Raft regions asynchronously in the background. Rebalancing happens at the storage layer and doesn't block SQL queries. Operator control over replica placement is granular.
- **CockroachDB**: Range rebalancing is automatic but happens through data movement across nodes. Large rebalancing operations (e.g., after scaling) can compete with production queries for network and disk I/O, impacting throughput.

**Operational complexity**
- **TiDB**: Operators tune TiKV (region size, store capacity) and TiFlash independently. Compute scaling is simple (add TiDB nodes), but storage scaling requires TiKV cluster management.
- **CockroachDB**: Simpler conceptually (nodes are peers), but multi-region operations require careful zone configuration. Balancing read/write latency against consistency guarantees is more constrained by the unified architecture.

### Key Takeaway

TiDB's compute-storage separation is optimized for workloads that need elastic compute scaling, low-friction OLTP+OLAP, and predictable multi-region replication. CockroachDB's distributed SQL design prioritizes simplicity and strong consistency but can impose latency and rebalancing overhead on geo-distributed and read-write-heavy workloads. The right choice depends on whether your workload prioritizes compute elasticity and analytical co-tenancy (TiDB) or unified architecture simplicity (CockroachDB).

## TiDB vs CockroachDB: Side-by-Side Comparison

| Criteria | TiDB | CockroachDB |
|----------|------|-------------|
| **Primary Use** | OLTP + HTAP hybrid workloads; MySQL-compatible distributed SQL; elastic scaling without manual sharding | OLTP + operational analytics; Postgres-compatible distributed SQL; multi-region resilience |
| **SQL / Protocol Compatibility** | MySQL 5.7 / 8.0 wire protocol; familiar SQL dialect for MySQL teams; most ORMs work directly | Postgres wire protocol; Postgres SQL dialect; tools built for Postgres ecosystem |
| **Consistency & Distributed Transactions** | ACID transactions with 2PC-inspired coordination; SI (Snapshot Isolation) or RC (Read Committed) modes; distributed transaction guarantees across TiKV regions | ACID transactions; serializable isolation by default; Raft-based consensus ensures consistency; strong transactional guarantees across nodes |
| **Scaling Model** | Horizontal scaling SQL database; compute (TiDB nodes) scales independently from storage (TiKV); automatic data partitioning—no manual sharding required | Horizontal scaling SQL database; ranges auto-split and rebalance; implicit partitioning—no manual sharding required |
| **HTAP Database Capability** | Native HTAP via TiFlash columnar storage; real-time OLAP queries on fresh OLTP data without ETL; TiFlash replicates from TiKV automatically | External analytics patterns; materialized views; streaming CDC for downstream OLAP; no native columnar replica |
| **Multi-Region Database Patterns** | Single-region HA via Raft; multi-region deployments require application-level routing or cross-region replicas; latency-sensitive workloads may need region-local writes | Multi-region built-in; ranges replicate across regions; Raft consensus adds cross-region latency; designed for geo-distributed resilience |
| **Operations & Reliability** | TiDB Cloud (managed) or self-managed on Kubernetes; zero-downtime online DDL / schema changes; PITR and snapshot backups; moderate operational complexity for self-managed | Cockroach Cloud (managed) or self-managed; zero-downtime schema changes; PITR; strong HA guarantees; managed service reduces operational burden |
| **Implementation / Migration Surface Area** | MySQL schema translates directly; most MySQL queries work without rewrite; ORMs (SQLAlchemy, Hibernate, etc.) work out-of-the-box; some ACID edge cases require validation | Postgres schema translates directly; Postgres queries mostly work; Postgres ORMs compatible; some TiDB-specific features require testing |
| **Pricing Model & Cost Drivers** | TiDB Cloud: compute nodes + storage + backup; self-managed: infrastructure costs + operational overhead; TiFlash adds columnar storage cost but avoids external OLAP tool licensing | Cockroach Cloud: compute + storage + multi-region replication; self-managed: infrastructure + ops; multi-region adds replication/bandwidth cost |

### Key Decision Points at a Glance

- **MySQL Migration Path**: TiDB wins for teams with large MySQL installations; minimal query rewrite.
- **HTAP Analytics**: TiDB with TiFlash eliminates separate analytics infrastructure; CockroachDB requires external OLAP stack.
- **Operational Simplicity**: CockroachDB managed service reduces ops friction; TiDB Cloud offers managed option but self-managed adds complexity.
- **Multi-Region Design**: CockroachDB is built for cross-region from day one; TiDB multi-region requires careful topology planning.
- **Database Sharding Avoidance**: Both eliminate manual database sharding and database partitioning burden; TiDB's compute-storage separation offers finer scaling granularity.

## TiDB vs CockroachDB: Distributed SQL at Scale

Both TiDB and CockroachDB are modern **Distributed SQL databases** that enable teams to scale beyond single-node MySQL or PostgreSQL. This guide compares them across architecture, compatibility, ACID transactions, HTAP analytics, multi-region design, operations, and total cost of ownership—so platform engineers and database leaders can make an informed choice.

**What you'll learn:**
- How TiDB's MySQL compatibility and TiFlash HTAP engine compare to CockroachDB's Postgres wire protocol and analytics patterns
- Why distributed transactions, consistency models, and failure-domain design matter for real-world workloads
- Scaling strategies: when you need manual database sharding vs when the database handles partitioning transparently
- Multi-region tradeoffs—locality, latency, write-path constraints, and consensus overhead
- Operational reality: online schema change (online DDL), upgrades, backups, and observability expectations
- Implementation roadmap: compatibility assessment, data migration, cutover patterns, and production readiness
- Pricing drivers and self-managed vs managed service cost profiles

**Updated:** February 2026 | **Category:** Distributed SQL, NewSQL, HTAP databases

## TiDB vs CockroachDB: Scale-Out Architectures and Scaling Models

### Understanding Horizontal Scaling in Distributed SQL

Both TiDB and CockroachDB are built as **distributed SQL databases** that scale horizontally—adding compute and storage nodes to handle larger workloads without manual intervention. However, their scaling architectures and operational models differ significantly.

**TiDB's scale-out model** separates compute from storage. TiDB uses **TiKV** as a distributed key-value storage layer and **TiFlash** as a columnar storage replica for analytics workloads. This independence means you can scale compute (SQL nodes) and storage (TiKV) independently, and add TiFlash replicas on-demand for HTAP analytics without affecting transactional performance. The separation simplifies capacity planning: if you need more query parallelism, add TiDB nodes; if you need more storage throughput, add TiKV nodes.

**CockroachDB's scale-out model** tightly couples compute and storage in each node. When you add a CockroachDB node, it participates in both query processing and data rebalancing. CockroachDB automatically rebalances data ranges across the cluster to maintain even distribution and optimize locality. This tight coupling can simplify some operational aspects but means scaling decisions affect both dimensions together.

### Database Partitioning vs Database Sharding: What You Manage vs What the Database Manages

Understanding the difference between **database partitioning** and **database sharding** is critical when evaluating scaling models:

- **Database partitioning** is a logical division of data within a single database or cluster—rows are split across ranges or ranges based on a key (range, list, or hash partition). The database itself manages partition placement and queries transparently. Both TiDB and CockroachDB use partitioning internally to distribute data across nodes.

- **Database sharding** is a manual, application-level distribution where different datasets live in separate database instances. The application logic routes queries to the correct shard based on a shard key. Sharding introduces complexity: each shard is independently managed, cross-shard joins are difficult, rebalancing is manual, and application code must handle shard logic.

**TiDB** abstracts away sharding entirely. You write SQL as if the database is a single instance; TiDB and TiKV handle all data distribution, rebalancing, and range placement transparently. If you add nodes, TiKV automatically rebalances ranges to distribute load. There is no application-level sharding logic to maintain.

**CockroachDB** similarly removes manual sharding from your application but uses its rebalancing system to maintain even distribution and locality across nodes. Like TiDB, you query it as a single logical database.

Both systems eliminate the operational burden of manual **database sharding**; the trade-off is that you must design your schema and queries with the system's partitioning strategy in mind (e.g., choosing appropriate primary keys and understanding hot-spot risks).

### Benchmarking Methodology: How to Compare Scaling Fairly

When evaluating TiDB vs CockroachDB scaling behavior, follow these methodological principles to avoid misleading conclusions:

1. **Define the workload clearly**: OLTP (transactional), OLAP (analytical), or mixed (HTAP). Scaling characteristics differ dramatically. TiFlash, for example, is designed to accelerate OLAP on TiDB without burdening the transactional layer; CockroachDB has no separate analytics replica, so large analytical queries compete with OLTP.

2. **Measure at production-like scale**: Benchmark with data volumes and concurrency that match your target environment. Small-scale tests (gigabytes, hundreds of connections) may not exhibit hot-spot contention or rebalancing behavior seen at terabyte+ scale.

3. **Include network and failure conditions**: Latency, packet loss, and node failures expose architectural differences. Single-datacenter benchmarks miss multi-region replication costs and consensus delays.

4. **Isolate scaling dimensions**: Test compute scaling (add query nodes) separately from storage scaling (add storage nodes). Measure how query latency, throughput, and rebalancing overhead change at each step.

5. **Track operational metrics**: Rebalancing time, memory usage, CPU utilization during scaling events, and recovery time after node failures often matter more than peak throughput.

6. **Validate with your own POC**: Vendor benchmarks can be optimized for the publishing company. Run representative queries, data volumes, and failure modes on your own infrastructure or a sandbox cloud environment.

7. **Avoid cherry-picking**: Present results across multiple workloads (e.g., small-transaction OLTP, large-scan OLAP, mixed) rather than a single "best-case" scenario.

For TiDB, pay attention to how TiKV rebalancing and TiFlash replica population affect query performance during scale-out. For CockroachDB, measure how range rebalancing and locality optimization impact read/write latency as the cluster grows. Both systems are designed to scale, but operational overhead and consistency trade-offs vary across conditions.

## HTAP Database Capabilities: Real-Time Analytics on Fresh Data

**TiDB HTAP with TiFlash** delivers real-time OLTP and OLAP workloads on a single platform. TiDB's compute layer handles transactional queries against TiKV (the distributed key-value store), while TiFlash—a columnar replica of your data—enables fast analytical queries without ETL. Data syncs from TiKV to TiFlash automatically, so your BI tools and operational dashboards query consistent, up-to-date information. This architecture eliminates the traditional "data warehouse lag" and simplifies your stack by removing the need for separate analytics databases.

**CockroachDB analytics approaches** rely on materialized views and external OLAP patterns. CockroachDB can materialize query results within the database or stream data to external systems (like data warehouses or Snowflake) for analytics workloads. This approach gives teams flexibility but requires more operational overhead to manage the separate analytics layer and handle data consistency between systems.

### Common HTAP Use Cases to Compare

**Fraud detection and risk management**: Real-time HTAP lets you flag suspicious transactions instantly by analyzing patterns (e.g., geographic anomalies, velocity checks) without waiting for batch processes. TiFlash's columnar design speeds up these analytical queries; with CockroachDB, you'd typically stream events to an external analytics system and accept slight latency.

**Operational intelligence**: Monitor business metrics (revenue, user signups, feature adoption) live without querying transactional tables directly. TiFlash separates read load from OLTP, preventing analytics queries from starving critical transactional workloads. CockroachDB can do this, but it requires more manual materialization and external tooling.

**Real-time reporting and dashboards**: Executive dashboards that refresh every minute or less. TiDB's HTAP model ensures reports reflect the latest committed data instantly. CockroachDB's external analytics pattern introduces pipeline lag (minutes to hours), which may or may not be acceptable for your use case.

### When HTAP Matters Most

Choose TiDB if you need **sub-minute analytics freshness** on transactional data with minimal operational complexity. Choose CockroachDB if you can **tolerate external analytics pipelines** and prefer flexibility in choosing your analytics engine.

# Multi-Region Database Reality Check: Latency, Locality, and Tradeoffs

Multi-region deployment is often positioned as a "must-have" for modern distributed databases, but the reality is more nuanced. Whether you actually need multi-region capability depends on your availability requirements, acceptable latency, and tolerance for cross-region write coordination overhead.

## Common Multi-Region Topologies and Which Teams Need Them

Three primary multi-region patterns exist:

**Single-Region HA (High Availability)**
- All data replicas within one region; read replicas or standby replicas in secondary availability zones
- **Best for:** Teams prioritizing latency and write performance; regulatory data residency requirements; teams without need for active-active failover across regions
- **Trade-off:** Limited protection against region-wide outages; requires planned failover to a warm standby region

**Multi-Region Reads (Read Replicas)**
- Primary writes stay in one region; read-only replicas in other regions via asynchronous replication
- **Best for:** Global read distribution with low read latency; acceptable eventual consistency for read-heavy workloads
- **Trade-off:** Stale reads; higher replication lag during write spikes; limited by primary region write throughput

**Multi-Region Writes (Active-Active)**
- Multiple regions accept writes simultaneously; consensus required across regions for durability
- **Best for:** True active-active resilience; zero-loss failover across regions
- **Trade-off:** Cross-region latency directly impacts write confirmation time; consensus overhead increases tail latency; network partitions require careful handling

## TiDB Multi-Region Patterns

TiDB supports multi-region deployments via its storage layer (TiKV) and compute layer separation:

- **Region topology via Placement Rules:** TiDB allows fine-grained replica placement across regions using Placement Rules, enabling hybrid topologies (e.g., 2 local replicas + 1 remote replica for quorum)
- **Read replicas:** TiFlash columnar replicas can be placed in remote regions for HTAP analytics without impacting primary write path
- **Asynchronous replication:** TiDB Binlog and Change Data Capture (CDC) enable async replication to secondary regions for read-heavy standby clusters
- **Write latency consideration:** Consensus-based writes across regions incur cross-region latency for each transaction commit; acceptable for most OLTP but notable for microsecond-sensitive use cases

## CockroachDB Multi-Region Patterns

CockroachDB is architecturally designed for multi-region from the ground up:

- **Geo-distributed consensus:** By default, replicas are spread across regions; write consensus requires quorum approval across regions
- **Implicit read replicas:** Non-voting replicas can be placed in remote regions to serve reads locally without participating in write consensus
- **Follower reads:** Reads can be served from any replica (including non-voters) with tunable staleness for improved local latency
- **Cross-region write latency:** Every write transaction incurs at least one cross-region round-trip for quorum; average write latency typically 100–200ms in a 3-region deployment

## Decision Rule: When "Multi-Region" Becomes a Requirement vs a Nice-to-Have

Use this framework to determine if multi-region deployment is truly needed:

**Multi-Region is a REQUIREMENT if:**
- Regulatory requirement: Data residency laws mandate replicas in specific regions
- True active-active failover needed: RPO (Recovery Point Objective) < 1 minute; RTO (Recovery Time Objective) < 30 seconds across regions
- Global read latency SLA: Read operations must complete within ~50ms from any region
- Zero-loss durability mandate: Cannot tolerate data loss even during planned region failover

**Multi-Region is NICE-TO-HAVE if:**
- Acceptable RTO > 1 hour with planned failover process
- Read traffic concentrated in primary region; remote reads serve <10% of traffic
- Write latency sensitive (p99 < 100ms): Cross-region write coordination will exceed target
- Disaster recovery only; passive standby acceptable

**Consider Single-Region HA instead if:**
- Application latency sensitive (p99 write < 50ms required)
- Compliance allows reads from other regions but writes must stay local
- Cost is primary constraint; multi-region replication adds 2–3x infrastructure
- Team lacks operational expertise for distributed consensus debugging

## Latency Impact: Real Numbers

Cross-region round-trip latency (approximate, inter-cloud region):
- Same region (3 AZ HA): 1–5ms
- Adjacent regions (e.g., us-east to us-west): 50–80ms
- Continental (e.g., US to EU): 120–160ms
- Intercontinental (e.g., US to APAC): 200–300ms

For multi-region writes requiring consensus, add these latencies to your write path. A 100ms cross-region latency means each transaction confirmation waits ~100ms minimum for remote acknowledgment.

## Practical Multi-Region Evaluation Checklist

**Before committing to multi-region:**
1. Document RPO/RTO requirements explicitly ("5 minutes" vs "5 seconds")
2. Measure current write latency p99; add 100–200ms for cross-region overhead
3. Test read-from-replica staleness impact on application logic
4. Plan network failure scenarios (partition, degraded cross-region link)
5. Cost-model: multi-region typically 2–3x single-region infrastructure
6. Validate that multi-region solves the stated problem (e.g., don't add multi-region for "high availability" if single-region HA suffices)

Many teams deploy single-region HA initially and migrate to multi-region only when scale, compliance, or true active-active requirements force the decision.

## Operations & Reliability: Upgrades, Backups, and Online Schema Change

Operational friction is often a deciding factor for platform teams and database leaders evaluating distributed SQL systems. This section covers the practical day-2 realities: zero-downtime schema changes, upgrade strategies, backup/restore capabilities, and observability.

### Zero-Downtime Schema Changes – Online DDL

Traditional SQL databases require downtime or complex dual-write patterns to alter schemas at scale. TiDB supports **online DDL** (online schema change), allowing you to add columns, create indexes, or modify table structures without blocking reads or writes. This is critical for high-availability systems where maintenance windows are not negotiable.

**Why it matters:**
- Eliminates coordinated downtime windows
- Reduces risk of human error during cutover
- Allows continuous CI/CD pipelines without database lockouts
- Simplifies compliance and SLA management

CockroachDB also supports online schema changes through its distributed SQL foundation, though implementation details and performance characteristics may differ. [Learn more about online DDL and zero-downtime schema changes](https://www.pingcap.com/blog/effective-online-ddl-database-schema-changes-zero-downtime/).

### Upgrades and Maintenance Windows – What to Expect

**TiDB:**
- Rolling upgrade capability (upgrade nodes without full cluster restart)
- Backward compatibility across minor versions
- Typical upgrade: 15–30 minutes per cluster (varies by scale and config)
- Self-managed clusters require operator/DBA coordination; TiDB Cloud handles upgrades automatically

**CockroachDB:**
- Also supports rolling upgrades
- Version compatibility windows (typically support 2–3 recent major versions)
- Managed CockroachDB handles upgrades; self-hosted requires manual coordination

### Backup/Restore, PITR, and Disaster Recovery Considerations

**TiDB:**
- Incremental backup support via Backup & Restore (BR) tool
- Point-in-time recovery (PITR) enables rollback to any moment in transaction history
- Backup to S3, GCS, or NFS
- Cross-region backup for DR
- Recovery RPO: minutes (with PITR); RTO: depends on data size and network

**CockroachDB:**
- Full and incremental backup options
- PITR available in enterprise version
- Backup to cloud storage (S3, GCS, Azure)
- Multi-region replication for inherent HA (reduces traditional backup dependency)
- Recovery RTO: typically lower due to active replica promotion

**Key tradeoff:** CockroachDB's multi-region replication can reduce backup frequency but increases operational complexity and cross-region write latency.

### Observability and Day-2 Operations Checklist

Successful database operations depend on visibility. Both systems provide metrics, logs, and alerting, but at different operational layers.

**TiDB observability:**
- Prometheus metrics for all components (TiDB, TiKV, TiFlash, PD)
- Grafana dashboards (pre-built for common operations)
- Slow query log for identifying problematic queries
- TiDB Cloud console for managed clusters
- Key metrics: QPS, latency, storage usage, replication lag

**CockroachDB observability:**
- Built-in UI with cluster health and node status
- Prometheus-compatible metrics export
- SQL activity monitoring
- Cloud console for managed deployments

**Day-2 operations checklist:**
- [ ] **Alert policy:** Define SLO thresholds (latency p99, error rate, storage utilization)
- [ ] **Capacity planning:** Monitor growth; plan scale-out timeline
- [ ] **Replication health:** Track replica lag and peer connectivity
- [ ] **Query performance:** Profile slow queries; index optimization
- [ ] **Backup validation:** Test restore procedures monthly
- [ ] **Security updates:** Subscribe to vulnerability notifications
- [ ] **Runbooks:** Document common failures (node down, network partition, disk full)
- [ ] **On-call rotation:** Ensure 24/7 coverage for critical systems
- [ ] **Performance baselines:** Establish baseline metrics for anomaly detection
- [ ] **Cost tracking:** Monitor compute, storage, and egress per environment

**Managed vs. self-managed trade-off:**
- **TiDB Cloud** or **CockroachDB Cloud:** Vendor handles upgrades, backups, and scaling; ops team focuses on application-level SLOs
- **Self-managed:** Full control but requires dedicated infrastructure expertise, 24/7 monitoring, and runbook rigor

For teams without deep database infrastructure experience, managed services reduce operational burden. For organizations with strong DBA teams, self-managed deployments offer cost savings and customization.

## Deployment Patterns: Self-Managed vs Managed Service

**TiDB** offers flexibility across deployment models:

- **TiDB Cloud**: A fully managed, multi-tenant service (similar to CockroachDB Cloud) that handles provisioning, scaling, backups, and upgrades automatically. Ideal for teams prioritizing operational simplicity and faster time-to-value.
- **Self-managed TiDB**: Deploy on Kubernetes or bare metal for maximum control over infrastructure, compliance, and cost optimization. Requires dedicated operational expertise but suits organizations with strict data residency or regulatory requirements.

**CockroachDB** similarly provides both managed (CockroachDB Cloud) and self-managed options, though the self-managed path often requires more infrastructure planning.

## Data Movement & CDC (Change Data Capture)

Both platforms support streaming data pipelines and real-time analytics:

- **TiDB**: Native CDC (TiCDC) streams changes to Kafka, cloud storage (S3, GCS), or other data systems with minimal latency. Commonly used for real-time analytics pipelines, operational intelligence, and cross-database replication.
- **CockroachDB**: Supports change feeds to Kafka and cloud object storage; integration patterns are similar but ecosystem tooling depth varies.

When evaluating, consider:
- **Latency tolerance**: Sub-second CDC is critical for fraud detection, compliance alerting, or real-time dashboards.
- **Consumer patterns**: Do you need ordering guarantees? Multi-destination fan-out?
- **Compliance & retention**: How long must change logs be retained?

## Developer & Operational Tooling Expectations

Platform teams should plan for:

### Developer Experience
- **MySQL compatibility** (TiDB) vs **Postgres compatibility** (CockroachDB) affects driver selection, ORM behavior, and SQL feature adoption.
- Both support popular ORMs (GORM, SQLAlchemy, Hibernate), but edge cases in connection pooling, transaction retries, or stored procedures require validation.
- TiDB's MySQL wire protocol simplifies migration for teams already using MySQL tools and practices.

### Operational Tooling
- **Observability**: Prometheus metrics, slow query logs, and distributed tracing (Jaeger) integration are expected. TiDB provides TiDB Dashboard for cluster health and performance diagnostics.
- **Backup & recovery**: Automated snapshots, point-in-time recovery (PITR), and cross-region replication tooling should be built into your runbook.
- **Scaling & rebalancing**: Auto-scaling policies, maintenance windows, and what happens during node failures must be documented and tested before production.
- **Alerting**: Define SLOs for query latency, replication lag, and storage utilization; configure alert thresholds accordingly.
- **Cost visibility**: Both platforms require metering for compute (CPU, memory), storage, and data egress. Set up cost attribution by team or application to avoid bill shock.

### Integration with Existing Stack
- **Kubernetes**: Both TiDB and CockroachDB use Kubernetes Operators for declarative management, fitting modern DevOps practices.
- **CI/CD**: Schema migrations, backup testing, and canary deployments should integrate with your GitOps workflow.
- **Data pipelines**: ETL/ELT tools (dbt, Apache Airflow, Fivetran) must support your chosen database. TiDB's MySQL compatibility often simplifies tool integration.

Start with a **deployment checklist**: infrastructure provisioning, network security, backup automation, failover procedures, and on-call runbooks. A successful deployment requires alignment between platform, application, and ops teams at least 4–6 weeks before production launch.

## Implementation / Migration Plan - How to De-Risk the Move

Moving to a new distributed SQL database requires careful planning across compatibility, data movement, cutover strategy, and validation. The following five-step approach helps platform teams and architects minimize risk, validate assumptions in a controlled environment, and achieve a smooth production transition.

### Step 1 — Compatibility Assessment: Schema and Queries

Before committing resources to a full migration, conduct a thorough audit of your existing schema and workload.

**What to assess:**
- **Schema compatibility**: Review table definitions, indexes, constraints, and data types. Identify any unsupported features or syntax differences between your current database and the target (TiDB or CockroachDB).
- **Query patterns**: Export a representative sample of production queries (OLTP, reporting, batch jobs). Test them against the target system to catch dialect differences, missing functions, or performance regressions early.
- **Application code**: Review ORM configurations, connection pooling, transaction handling, and error-handling logic. Verify that drivers and client libraries are available and tested.
- **Third-party integrations**: Catalog all tools, dashboards, ETL pipelines, and monitoring systems that connect to your database. Check compatibility with the target's wire protocol and APIs.

**Deliverables:**
- Compatibility matrix (schema, queries, drivers, integrations)
- List of required code changes or workarounds
- Estimated effort for adapter code or feature gaps

---

### Step 2 — Data Migration and Validation Strategy

Once compatibility is cleared, plan how to move your data safely and verify correctness.

**Migration approaches:**
- **Logical dump/restore**: Use mysqldump (for TiDB) or pg_dump (for CockroachDB) for smaller datasets. Suitable for offline migrations or testing.
- **Change Data Capture (CDC)**: For near-zero downtime, set up CDC to stream changes from your source database to the target in real time. TiDB supports [TiCDC](https://docs.pingcap.com/tidb/stable/ticdc-overview); verify CockroachDB's CDC capabilities for your source system.
- **Parallel load tools**: Use open-source tools (e.g., go-mysql-elasticsearch, Kafka Connect) or vendor tooling to parallelize data ingestion.

**Validation approach:**
- **Row count and checksum verification**: After initial load, compare row counts and checksums (e.g., `MD5(*)` on key columns) between source and target to catch missing or corrupted rows.
- **Spot-check sampling**: Randomly query high-volume tables and compare results across databases.
- **Application-level validation**: Run your application's data validation layer (if it exists) against the target to ensure business logic correctness.
- **Reconciliation reports**: Generate reports on differences and categorize them (expected schema changes, type coercions, null handling).

**Deliverables:**
- Migration runbook with step-by-step procedures
- Validation queries and automated checks
- Rollback plan if validation fails

---

### Step 3 — Cutover Approach: Dual-Write vs Planned Cutover

Choose a cutover strategy that balances risk tolerance and downtime requirements.

**Dual-write approach (lower risk, higher operational complexity):**
- Run both databases in parallel for a period (1–4 weeks typical).
- Application writes to both the source and target simultaneously.
- Continuously validate that both systems remain in sync.
- Monitor for any write-path divergence or application errors.
- **Advantages**: Easy rollback; can catch incompatibilities before full cutover.
- **Disadvantages**: Increased operational overhead, eventual consistency challenges, requires coordinated writes in application code.

**Planned cutover approach (faster, requires higher confidence):**
- Use CDC or a final snapshot to bring the target fully up to date.
- Stop application writes to the source at a planned maintenance window.
- Perform final verification that target is current.
- Switch application connection strings to the target.
- Begin accepting production traffic on the target.
- **Advantages**: Simpler operationally; faster time to production.
- **Disadvantages**: Requires brief downtime and higher pre-cutover validation confidence.

**Hybrid approach:**
- Perform dual-write validation for a shorter window (3–7 days) with lower traffic volume.
- Use CDC for the final data sync during cutover.
- Combine confidence-building with acceptable maintenance windows.

**Deliverables:**
- Cutover timeline and communication plan
- Application configuration changes (connection strings, driver setup)
- Runbook for switching traffic (with rollback steps)

---

### Step 4 — Performance and Failure Testing Runbook

Validate that the target system can handle production workloads under normal and degraded conditions.

**Performance testing:**
- **Load profile matching**: Replay production traffic patterns (query mix, concurrency, data volume) against the target using tools like HammerDB, SysBench, or custom load generators.
- **Latency and throughput**: Measure p50, p95, p99 query latencies and transactions per second (TPS). Compare against baseline and ensure acceptable SLAs.
- **Long-running workload stability**: Run tests for 24+ hours to catch memory leaks, connection pool issues, or rebalancing effects.
- **Hotspot identification**: Identify hot rows or indexes under load and test conflict resolution and lock contention handling.

**Failure and recovery testing:**
- **Single-node failure**: Stop one database node and verify that queries succeed and failover is transparent to the application.
- **Network partition**: Simulate a network split and test consistency guarantees and recovery.
- **Long transactions**: Test behavior under long-running transactions with high abort/retry rates.
- **Connection pool exhaustion**: Verify graceful handling when application connection pools are exhausted.
- **Backup and restore**: Perform a full backup, simulate data loss, and verify point-in-time recovery (PITR).

**HTAP-specific testing (if relevant):**
- If using TiFlash or similar OLAP capabilities, test that analytical queries see fresh data without blocking OLTP.
- Verify freshness SLAs (e.g., "analytics data is ≤5 minutes stale").

**Deliverables:**
- Performance testing report with baseline comparison
- Failure recovery procedures and timing data
- SLA validation sign-off

---

### Step 5 — Production Readiness Checklist

Before switching all traffic, complete this checklist to ensure operational confidence.

**Application and code:**
- [ ] All schema migrations applied and tested on target
- [ ] Application code changes deployed and integration-tested
- [ ] Error handling and retry logic validated for target-specific errors
- [ ] Monitoring and logging instrumentation verified

**Database configuration:**
- [ ] High availability / replication configured and tested
- [ ] Backup schedule enabled and tested
- [ ] Automated alerts configured for key metrics (CPU, memory, disk, slow queries)
- [ ] Query optimization (indexes, statistics) completed
- [ ] Connection pooling and timeout settings tuned

**Operations:**
- [ ] On-call runbooks created for common issues
- [ ] Escalation procedures and contact list defined
- [ ] Rollback procedure tested and documented
- [ ] Communication plan in place (incident response, status pages)
- [ ] Support contract or SLA agreement signed (if using managed service)

**Observability:**
- [ ] Dashboards and queries set up in monitoring system
- [ ] Alerting thresholds defined for SLOs
- [ ] Slow query log enabled and reviewed
- [ ] Transaction conflict / retry rates baselined

**Compliance and security:**
- [ ] Encryption in transit and at rest configured
- [ ] Database user permissions and roles aligned with least-privilege policy
- [ ] Audit logging enabled (if required)
- [ ] Data residency and compliance requirements met

**Deliverables:**
- Completed checklist signed off by platform/ops and architecture teams
- Production runbook and incident response guide
- Go/no-go decision criteria defined

---

## Key Success Factors

- **Start compatibility assessment early**: Don't wait until full data migration is planned to discover unsupported SQL features.
- **Invest in validation**: The cost of catching issues in a test environment is far lower than the cost of production incidents.
- **Automate where possible**: Automated validation, performance tests, and failure scenarios are repeatable and reproducible.
- **Plan for contingency**: Even with careful planning, problems surface in production. Keep rollback procedures tested and on-call teams trained.
- **Phased rollout**: If possible, migrate lower-criticality workloads first to gain operational confidence before moving critical systems.

## Pricing & Cost Drivers - What Actually Moves the Bill

Database pricing can be deceptively complex. What matters for your total cost of ownership (TCO) is understanding **which levers actually move the bill** and how each system's consumption model aligns with your workload pattern.

### TiDB: Self-Managed vs TiDB Cloud Pricing Model

**Self-Managed TiDB** operates on an open-source model with no licensing fees. You pay only for infrastructure:

* **Compute**: TiDB nodes (SQL layer), TiKV nodes (distributed KV storage), and TiFlash nodes (columnar replica for HTAP) each consume compute resources you provision.
* **Storage**: TiKV and TiFlash storage scales independently, so you can tune columnar replica capacity separately from your transactional layer.
* **Operations**: You manage infrastructure, patching, upgrades, and monitoring—reducing vendor lock-in but increasing operational overhead.

**TiDB Cloud** (PingCAP's managed service) uses a consumption-based pricing model:

* **Compute per cluster**: Billed by vCPU-hours allocated to TiDB, TiKV, and TiFlash tiers.
* **Storage**: Separate tiers for TiKV storage and TiFlash storage, both metered by GB stored and accessed.
* **Backup and point-in-time recovery (PITR)**: Additional charges for PITR retention and snapshot egress.
* **Data transfer (egress)**: Cross-region replication and outbound data transfer incur per-GB fees.
* **Support tier**: Standard, professional, and enterprise support levels add recurring costs.
* **Multi-region premium**: Running clusters across regions adds compute and transfer costs; single-region deployments are more cost-efficient.

For most teams, TiDB Cloud pricing is predictable when workload profiles are stable. The trade-off is lower operational burden vs. higher per-unit compute cost compared to self-managed infrastructure.

### CockroachDB: Pricing Model Overview

**CockroachDB Self-Hosted** (open-source) follows a similar model to TiDB—no licensing, infrastructure only. You provision and pay for your own compute and storage.

**CockroachDB Cloud** (Cockroach Labs' managed service) offers:

* **Compute**: Billed by vCPU-hours per cluster node.
* **Storage**: Per-GB-month charges for total data stored (no separate analytical tier).
* **Egress**: Data transfer out of the cloud region, charged per GB.
* **Backup retention**: PITR and backup storage add to monthly costs.
* **Support**: Professional and dedicated support plans available.
* **Multi-region setup**: Running clusters across regions increases node costs; single-region is more economical.

CockroachDB Cloud's pricing structure is generally comparable to TiDB Cloud on a per-vCPU basis, though the lack of a separate columnar storage tier (like TiFlash) means HTAP workloads often require external analytics solutions—which may offset pricing parity.

### Cost Driver Checklist

When evaluating TCO for either platform, audit these cost levers:

**Compute**
* How many vCPU-hours does your workload consume per month?
* Does the workload spike seasonally, or is it steady-state?
* Are you running single-region or multi-region?
* Do you need a separate columnar replica for analytics (TiFlash with TiDB), or is your OLAP handled externally?

**Storage**
* How much raw data do you store in your transactional layer?
* If using TiDB Cloud with TiFlash, what's the size of your columnar replica?
* Do you anticipate rapid data growth, requiring reserved capacity discounts?

**Backups & PITR**
* How long do you retain point-in-time recovery windows (days / weeks / months)?
* What's your snapshot egress frequency?
* Do you need cross-region backup copies for disaster recovery?

**Data Transfer / Egress**
* How much data moves out of your database region per month (analytics, replication, app serving)?
* Are you running multi-region, triggering inter-region transfer costs?
* Do you have external consumers (data warehouses, caches) pulling data via CDC?

**Regions & Topology**
* Single-region deployments are always cheaper than multi-region.
* Multi-region consensus adds latency and cost; weigh HA/DR value against per-GB transfer charges.
* Regional failover standby clusters incur full compute costs even when idle.

**Support Tier**
* Standard support is included in cloud offerings; professional and enterprise tiers add 10–30% recurring costs.
* Self-managed deployments require internal expertise or third-party support contracts (external cost not visible in list pricing).

**Reserved Capacity & Discounts**
* Both vendors offer commitment discounts (1-year, 3-year) for predictable workloads.
* Self-managed deployments can lock in low infrastructure costs if you pre-purchase compute capacity.

**Key Question for Your Team**: Are you optimizing for lowest unit cost (self-managed, existing infrastructure), operational simplicity (managed cloud), or balanced TCO? The answer shifts which platform's pricing model favors your budget.

## TiDB vs CockroachDB: Key Tradeoffs at a Glance

**TiDB** and **CockroachDB** both solve the core challenge of scaling SQL workloads across multiple nodes while maintaining ACID transaction guarantees. However, they make fundamentally different architectural choices that create distinct operational and compatibility tradeoffs:

### TiDB Tradeoffs

**Pros:**
- **MySQL compatibility**: Drop-in replacement for most MySQL workloads; existing drivers, ORMs, and SQL dialect knowledge transfer directly
- **Native HTAP via TiFlash**: Columnar replica layer enables real-time analytics on fresh OLTP data without separate ETL pipelines
- **Elastic scaling without manual sharding**: TiKV distributed storage handles data partitioning automatically; application teams avoid shard key design and rebalancing complexity
- **ACID transactions across regions**: Distributed transaction coordination via Percolator consensus model provides strong consistency guarantees
- **Zero-downtime schema changes**: Online DDL implementation allows production deployments without maintenance windows

**Cons / Tradeoffs:**
- **Write-heavy multi-region latency**: Cross-region writes require consensus participation from remote regions, increasing tail latency in geographically dispersed deployments
- **Operational learning curve**: TiKV and TiFlash introduce new components; platform teams must learn cluster operations, scaling behavior, and failure modes outside MySQL ecosystem
- **Lower ecosystem maturity for Postgres users**: No native Postgres wire protocol support; teams standardized on Postgres tooling face migration effort
- **Cost scales with storage footprint**: TiFlash columnar replicas duplicate data; analytics-heavy workloads increase storage and replication costs

---

### CockroachDB Tradeoffs

**Pros:**
- **Postgres compatibility**: Wire-protocol compatibility enables adoption of Postgres tooling, drivers, and SQL dialects; simpler onboarding for Postgres-native teams
- **Production-hardened multi-region**: Built-in lease-based read locality and write-path optimization reduce cross-region consensus friction compared to naive replicated architectures
- **Unified SQL engine**: Simpler operational model; one consistent SQL dialect across all deployments (no separate columnar layer)
- **Strong consistency by default**: Geo-partitioned tables can be co-located with read traffic; explicit locality control available for performance tuning

**Cons / Tradeoffs:**
- **No native HTAP layer**: Requires external OLAP tools, materialized view patterns, or CDC to separate analytics workloads; increases stack complexity
- **Manual data placement for scale**: Schema design and table partitioning become explicit operational concerns; teams must actively manage shard topology
- **MySQL incompatibility**: Existing MySQL applications require syntax translation, driver changes, and ORM validation; migration effort is higher for MySQL-native teams
- **Limited columnar acceleration**: Analytical queries against transactional tables incur full table scans or index lookups; no performance-optimized analytical engine
- **Rebalancing overhead at scale**: Adding nodes or regions triggers rebalancing; clusters beyond 100+ nodes see noticeable performance impact during rebalancing windows

---

### Decision Drivers

**Choose TiDB if:**
- Your application is MySQL-compatible and rewriting is not acceptable
- Real-time analytics (HTAP) is a core requirement, and you want to avoid separate analytics infrastructure
- Auto-scaling without manual sharding is a priority
- Your team has Kubernetes experience and can operate distributed storage systems

**Choose CockroachDB if:**
- Your team standardizes on Postgres, and compatibility with the Postgres ecosystem is non-negotiable
- You operate globally and need deterministic multi-region performance with explicit locality control
- Your workload is primarily transactional (OLTP), and analytics is a secondary concern or handled externally
- You prefer a unified SQL engine with minimal operational components

---

### Sourcing & Validation

All claims reflect documented architectural design (TiDB's [distributed transaction model](https://www.pingcap.com/blog/distributed-transactions-tidb/), [HTAP capabilities via TiFlash](https://www.pingcap.com/blog/harnessing-the-power-of-htap-databases/), and [online schema change zero-downtime behavior](https://www.pingcap.com/blog/effective-online-ddl-database-schema-changes-zero-downtime/)), and CockroachDB's published documentation on multi-region topology and Postgres compatibility. Platform teams should validate specific tradeoffs in a proof-of-concept using representative workload patterns and failure injection testing.

## Who Should Choose Which? – Decision Matrix

| Situation | TiDB | CockroachDB |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| **Best-fit by compatibility needs** | Choose TiDB if your team standardizes on MySQL, uses MySQL drivers/ORMs (JDBC, Go sql, Python mysqlconnector), or needs minimal app rewrites. TiDB's MySQL wire protocol and syntax compatibility means fewer surprises during migration. | Choose CockroachDB if your stack is Postgres-native—Postgres drivers (psycopg2, pq, GORM with Postgres dialect), existing Postgres tooling, and SQL patterns port more directly to CockroachDB's Postgres compatibility layer. |
| **Best-fit by workload** | Choose TiDB if you need both OLTP and real-time analytics on the same data without external ETL. TiFlash (columnar replica layer on TiKV) enables HTAP database patterns—fraud detection, operational dashboards, and reporting queries run fresh without denormalization or dual writes. | Choose CockroachDB if your workload is primarily OLTP with occasional analytical queries, or if you can tolerate external analytics stacks (data warehouse, BI tool). CockroachDB supports materialized views but lacks TiDB's tight HTAP integration. |
| **Best-fit by operating model** | Choose TiDB if you prefer managed infrastructure: **TiDB Cloud** (fully managed distributed SQL on AWS/GCP/Azure) removes operational overhead—backup, upgrades, scaling, online schema change handled by PingCAP. Ideal for platform teams avoiding Kubernetes/infrastructure complexity. | Choose CockroachDB if you have strong Kubernetes or cloud-native operations expertise, or if CockroachDB's managed offering (Cockroach Cloud) fits your region/compliance requirements. Self-managed CockroachDB requires more operational investment than TiDB Cloud. |
| **Best-fit by multi-region priorities** | Choose TiDB if you need single-region HA first, with optional multi-region expansion later. TiDB's architecture (compute + TiKV storage separation) scales within a region efficiently. Multi-region deployments exist but require careful latency/consensus planning—best for read-mostly or well-partitioned write patterns. | Choose CockroachDB if multi-region writes and global load-balancing are hard requirements from day one. CockroachDB's architecture favors distributed consensus across regions, though cross-region write latency and rebalancing overhead remain operational tradeoffs. |

### How to Use This Matrix

1. **Start with compatibility**: Does your team standardize on MySQL or Postgres? This eliminates half the field immediately.
2. **Layer in workload**: Do you need HTAP (TiDB + TiFlash advantage) or is OLTP-only sufficient?
3. **Consider operations**: Managed (TiDB Cloud) vs self-managed / Kubernetes (CockroachDB, or self-managed TiDB)?
4. **Validate multi-region needs**: Are they real today, or a future nice-to-have? This shapes architecture and initial deployment region count.

**Recommendation**: Schedule a short architecture workshop with your team to score each row. The winner often emerges quickly once compatibility + workload + operational model align.

items.q: Is TiDB a drop-in replacement for MySQL?
items.a: TiDB is MySQL-compatible at the protocol and SQL dialect level, making it suitable for many MySQL workloads without extensive rewrites. However, it is not a true drop-in replacement—you should validate application-specific SQL features, transaction patterns, and timing assumptions (e.g., AUTO_INCREMENT behavior, implicit type coercions) in a POC before production deployment. Consult the [TiDB MySQL Compatibility Guide](https://www.pingcap.com/tidb/cloud/) for detailed feature coverage.

items.q: How does TiDB handle distributed transactions and ACID transactions?
items.a: TiDB provides ACID guarantees across distributed nodes using a two-phase commit protocol coordinated by the Placement Driver (PD), ensuring isolation and durability even when data spans multiple machines. Transactions are isolated using multi-version concurrency control (MVCC), and TiDB's distributed transaction model guarantees strong consistency without sacrificing availability. For deep-dive details on how distributed transactions work at scale, see [ACID transactions in a distributed SQL database (TiDB)](https://www.pingcap.com/blog/distributed-transactions-tidb/).

items.q: What does HTAP mean, and how does TiFlash work?
items.a: HTAP stands for Hybrid Transactional/Analytical Processing—the ability to run both OLTP (transactional) and OLAP (analytical) workloads on the same database without maintaining separate systems or ETL pipelines. TiFlash is a columnar storage engine that replicates data from TiKV (TiDB's row-oriented KV store) in columnar format, enabling fast analytical queries while keeping analytics data synchronized with transactional writes. This eliminates the need for separate data warehouses for real-time reporting and fraud detection. Learn more in [HTAP database fundamentals and TiDB HTAP use cases](https://www.pingcap.com/blog/harnessing-the-power-of-htap-databases/).

items.q: Do I need database sharding or database partitioning with TiDB?
items.a: TiDB eliminates the need for manual database sharding—the distributed architecture automatically handles data distribution and rebalancing across nodes without application logic changes. TiDB does support table partitioning for specific use cases (e.g., time-series data retention policies), but this is optional and different from application-level sharding. See [Database sharding explained (and when to avoid it)](https://www.pingcap.com/blog/database-sharding-defined/) for a detailed comparison of when partitioning may be useful versus eliminating sharding entirely.

items.q: How do online DDL / online schema changes work in TiDB?
items.a: TiDB supports online DDL operations that allow schema changes (adding columns, creating indexes, modifying constraints) without blocking reads or writes to the table. The distributed architecture enables metadata changes to be propagated dynamically to all nodes, and TiDB uses a two-phase schema change protocol to ensure consistency during transitions. This eliminates maintenance windows and the operational risk of long-running ALTER TABLE operations. For implementation details and best practices, see [Online DDL / online schema change with zero downtime](https://www.pingcap.com/blog/effective-online-ddl-database-schema-changes-zero-downtime/).

items.q: How should I evaluate TiDB vs CockroachDB performance for my workload?
items.a: Start with a fair benchmark that isolates your actual workload: define realistic schemas, query mixes, concurrency profiles, and failure scenarios (e.g., node loss, network partitions). Run the same test against both systems using their recommended configurations and equivalent hardware; measure latency, throughput, and consistency under load and failure conditions rather than relying on vendor benchmarks. Build a small POC with your application code, real data volumes, and production-like topology—this will reveal compatibility gaps, operational friction, and true performance characteristics that generic benchmarks cannot capture.

props.content: Both TiDB and CockroachDB are production-grade distributed SQL databases built to scale horizontally while maintaining ACID transactions, but they differ significantly in compatibility, analytics capabilities, and operational model. **Choose TiDB if your team values MySQL compatibility, wants built-in HTAP analytics via TiFlash without external ETL, and prefers elastic scaling without manual database sharding; choose CockroachDB if you're invested in the Postgres ecosystem, prioritize multi-region write-heavy patterns, and have the operational capacity for custom multi-region tuning.**

props.title: Try TiDB Cloud for AI Apps
props.subtitle: Free serverless tier — no credit card required. Includes vector search, SQL, and TiFlash columnar analytics.
